query:
Nestled in eastern China's Zhejiang Province, Hangzhou has long enchanted the world with its timeless beauty, a city where West Lake's mist-shrouded bridges and ancient pagodas coexist harmoniously with cutting-edge tech hubs and sustainable urban developments. Known as "the most beautiful and luxurious city in the world" by Marco Polo, Hangzhou in 2025 stands as a compelling model of how traditional heritage and modern innovation can converge to create a sustainable, prosperous metropolis.
At the heart of Hangzhou's allure lies West Lake, a UNESCO World Heritage Site whose serene waters reflect willow-lined shores and iconic landmarks like the Broken Bridge and Leifeng Pagoda. But the city's natural charm extends beyond this historic water body. In 2025, Hangzhou is preparing to host the fifth World Biosphere Reserve Congress (WBRC) from September 22 to 25, a landmark event marking the first time this prestigious gathering has come to Asia. Expected to draw 4,000 delegates from over 150 countries, the congress will focus on "Shaping a Sustainable Future for Humans and Nature," with Hangzhou set to发布 the Hangzhou Declaration and outline a decade-long strategic action plan for global ecological protection. This international recognition underscores the city's commitment to balancing urban growth with environmental stewardship—a philosophy embodied in its海绵城市 (sponge city) initiatives, which have transformed 418.66 square kilometers of built-up area to absorb and utilize 75% of rainfall locally, earning it an "A" rating in China's national sponge city assessments.
Hangzhou's urban landscape is undergoing a remarkable transformation through its city renewal programs, which prioritize green development and livability. The city has revitalized 355 urban villages since 2016, converting once-dilapidated areas into modern communities with ample green spaces. Take Linghuo Town's Huilin Innovation Park, for example: a former woolen mill has been transformed into a 18,000-square-meter eco-industrial hub housing 301 enterprises, including 3 national high-tech companies, generating an annual output value of 2.424 billion yuan. Similarly, Binjiang District's Changhe Industrial Park has evolved from a traditional manufacturing zone into a smart industrial cluster, attracting 13 specialized and sophisticated enterprises and increasing land productivity significantly. These renewal projects not only enhance the quality of life for residents—evident in communities like Cuiyuan No.1, where a once-polluted pond now meets Grade III water standards—but also foster economic vitality by repurposing underutilized spaces.
Economically, Hangzhou demonstrates robust growth with a GDP of 1.13 trillion yuan ($157 billion) in the first half of 2025, representing a 5.5% year-on-year increase. The city's economic engine is driven by a dynamic mix of advanced manufacturing and modern services. High-tech industries grew by 8.5%, with new energy vehicle production surging by an impressive 117.8%. The tertiary sector, contributing over 70% to GDP growth, reflects a thriving service economy supported by rising resident incomes—per capita disposable income reached 44,709 yuan, with the urban-rural income gap continuing to narrow. Foreign trade also shows resilience, with exports to Belt and Road Initiative countries increasing by 19.9%, highlighting Hangzhou's role as a global trade hub.
What truly distinguishes Hangzhou is its ability to weave together the old and the new. Ancient temples like Lingyin Temple, with its thousand-year-old Buddhist statues, stand just a short drive from futuristic tech parks. The Grand Canal, a UNESCO World Heritage Site, now features revitalized waterfronts where locals and tourists alike enjoy cultural performances and waterfront dining. As Hangzhou prepares to welcome the world to the WBRC, it embodies the spirit of sustainable development—proving that a city can honor its past while leading the way toward a greener, more connected future. In Hangzhou, every corner tells a story of evolution, where natural beauty inspires innovation, and centuries of history lay the foundation for tomorrow's progress.
